perturbation.original_text: 4.1 For purposes of this Agreement, the term " Affiliate " shall mean any person which either directly or indirectly controls, is directly or indirectly controlled by, 3 or is under common control with a Party and, for purposes of t his definition, a person shall be deemed to "control" an entity if it owns or has the ability to vote, directly or indirectly, more than 50% of the voting securities or equivalent interest of that entity and a reference to an Affiliate shall include a refe rence to such Affiliate's employees, officers, directors , representatives and advis ers.
perturbation.changed_text: 4.1 For purposes of this Agreement, the term "Affiliate" shall mean any person which either directly or indirectly controls, is directly or indirectly controlled by, or is under common control with a Party. A person shall be deemed to "control" an entity if it owns or has the ability to vote, directly or indirectly, more than 25% of the voting securities or equivalent interest of that entity. A reference to an Affiliate shall only include a reference to such Affiliate's senior management.
perturbation.explanation: The definition of 'control' within the definition of 'Affiliate' has been changed from >50% to >25% and from employees, officers, directors, representatives and advisers to 'senior management'. This contradicts the standard legal understanding and common business practice of affiliate control.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Sarbanes-Oxley Act of 2002 defines control as greater than 50% ownership.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text defines 'control' as owning greater than 25% of voting securities for affiliate status, which is a substantially lower threshold than the common legal interpretation. This contradicts regulations (such as the Sarbanes-Oxley Act) that generally consider control to be associated with majority ownership (greater than 50%). It also limits what constitutes an affiliate to just senior management, which is very non-standard.

Corporate responsibility for financial reports (a) Regulations required The Commission shall, by rule, require, for each company filing periodic reports under section 78m(a) or 78o(d) of this title , that the principal executive officer or officers and the principal financial officer or officers, or persons performing similar functions, certify in each annual or quarterly r
perturbation.justification: The modified text redefines 'control' within the context of 'Affiliate' to include ownership of greater than 25% of voting securities, a significant deviation from the typical legal understanding of control as majority ownership (over 50%). This change, as explained, undermines the common legal interpretation of affiliate control. The modified text also limits the scope of what constitutes an affiliate to 'senior management,' excluding employees, officers, directors, representatives, and advisors. While Scraped Law Snippet 1, 15 USC 7241, primarily addresses corporate responsibility for financial reports and doesn't directly define 'control,' the Law Explanation asserts that the modification contradicts regulations, such as the Sarbanes-Oxley Act, which generally associate control with majority ownership. Though no definition is given in the snippet, it is implied that altering the threshold of ownership for 'control' may lead to legal complications regarding corporate responsibilities.

perturbation.original_text: 2.3 For purposes of this Agreement, " Confidential Information " shall include, without limitation, any information or other data relating to the Disclosing Party or any of its Affiliates, which is disclosed to the Receiving Party , whether prior to the conclusion of this Agreement, in terms of this Agreement or otherwise in connection with this Agreement or the Transaction (including but not limited to the Transaction itself and any negotiations in relation to the Transaction ), includ ing this Agreement, its contents and the negotiations leading up to the conclusion of this Agreement - 2.3.1 in whatever format whether written, oral or graphic, recorded or not (and if recorded, whether recorded in writing, on any electronic medium or 2 otherwis e howsoever) which is intended or by its nature could reasonably be expected to be confidential, which is disclosed or communicated to the Receiving Party by the Disclosing Party , its Affiliates or any of their respective employees, representatives , office rs, directors and advis ers, or which otherwise comes to the knowledge of the Receiving Party, by whatever means, before or after the date on which this Agreement is signed by the last Party signing it (" Signature Date"); and 2.3.2 which includes, without derog ating from the aforegoing, all financial information, marketing information, products, product information, drawings, plans and specificat ions, operational and risk methodologies, production and operating procedures, computer data, programs and source codes, pricing, pric e lists and purchasing policies, information relating to costs, sources of materials, business relationships, services, customers and customer list s (whether actual or potential); technical information, techniq ues, know -how, trade secrets, operating methods and procedures, electronic and manual systems; all intellectual property; contractual arrangements; training schemes and programs; information relating to the Disclosing Party 's strategic objectives and plann ing for both its existing and future business needs; the contractual business and financial arrangements between the Disclosing Party and other third Parties with whom they have business arrangements of whatever nature; all information specifically relate d to the business of the Disclosing Party which is not readily available to a competitor of the Disclosing Party in the ordinary course of business; the existence and contents of any process related documentation and all information or data relating thereto disclosed to the Receiving Party by the Disclosing Party, its Affiliates or any of their respective employ ees, representatives, officers, directors and advis ers.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.3 For purposes of this Agreement, "Confidential Information" shall only include information explicitly marked as 'Confidential' in writing at the time of disclosure. No other information, regardless of its nature or the circumstances of its disclosure, will be considered Confidential Information under this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: This drastically narrows the definition of Confidential Information, contradicting standard NDA practices where information is often considered confidential based on its nature and the context of disclosure, not solely on explicit written marking.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text requires that all confidential information be explicitly marked as confidential in writing. This contradicts the UTSA's definition of a trade secret, which includes information that derives independent economic value from not being generally known or readily ascertainable and is subject to efforts to maintain its confidentiality, regardless of whether it's explicitly labeled as such. The modified text significantly limits the scope of protection.

perturbation.justification: The modified text drastically narrows the definition of confidential information to only include information explicitly marked as 'Confidential' in writing. The Law Explanation states this contradicts the UTSA's definition of a trade secret. While the provided scraped law snippets are not valid to further confirm this explanation, the Perturbation Explanation also highlights that this change contradicts standard NDA practices, where confidentiality is determined not solely by explicit written marking, but also by the nature and context of disclosure. This deviation from industry-standard practices and the intent to protect trade secrets creates a legally significant contradiction, as it weakens the protection afforded to confidential information.

perturbation.original_text: 6.3 The Receiving Party shall comply with any requests in terms of 6.1 and/or 6.2 above within five "Business Days " (being any day other than a Saturday, Sunday or official public holiday in the Republic of South Africa) of the date of such a request.
perturbation.changed_text: 6.3 The Receiving Party shall comply with any requests in terms of 6.1 and/or 6.2 above within five "Business Days" (being any day other than a Saturday or Sunday) of the date of such a request.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'official public holiday in the Republic of South Africa', the term 'Business Days' now potentially includes public holidays, which contradicts the common understanding and legal interpretation of business days.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Definition of 'Business Day' in South African legislation, specifically the Public Holidays Act.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text omits official public holidays from the definition of 'Business Days'. This conflicts with the Public Holidays Act of South Africa, which recognizes specific days as public holidays. Consequently, obligating the receiving party to comply within a time frame that includes public holidays when such days are legally non-business days creates a contradiction. The act does not exactly define Business Days, however, it implies that Public Holidays are not business days.

perturbation.justification: The modified text removes 'official public holiday in the Republic of South Africa' from the definition of 'Business Days'. The Law Explanation states that this omission potentially includes public holidays, which are legally non-business days, creating a contradiction. Scraped Law Snippet 2, which is a Wikipedia entry, includes a list of Public Holidays in South Africa, implying that these days are distinct from normal business days. The perturbation explanation states that this change contradicts the common understanding and legal interpretation of business days. This misalignment of terminology, where 'Business Days' now potentially includes public holidays, creates a legal contradiction by deviating from a legally recognized understanding.

perturbation.original_text: 9.2 Neither Party shall be liable to the other Part y for any indirect or consequential damages , including loss of profits, business or anticipated savings or for any incident al damages arising from the breach of any of the provisions of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 9.2 Neither Party shall be liable to the other Party for any damages, including direct damages, arising from the breach of any of the provisions of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the exception for 'direct' damages, the clause now attempts to preclude recovery for direct damages resulting from a breach, which is generally not enforceable under contract law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General principles of contract law regarding direct damages.
perturbation.law_explanation: The changed text attempts to eliminate liability for all damages, including direct damages, which directly contradicts the fundamental principle of contract law that a party breaching a contract is liable for damages that directly result from the breach. The Restatement (Second) of Contracts outlines the standard measures of damages, which include direct losses.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: contract | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you contract A contract is an agreement between parties , creating mutual obligations that are enforceable by law . The basic elements required for the agreement to be a legally enforceable contract are: mutual assent , expressed by a valid offer and acceptance ; adequate consideration ; capacity ; and legality . In some states , elements of consideration can be satisfied by a valid substitute. Possible remedies for breach of contract include general damages , consequential damages , reliance damages , and specific performance . Background : Contracts are promises that the law will enforce. Contract law is generally governed by state common law , and while general overall contract law is common throughout the country, some specific court interpretations of a particular element of the contract may vary between the states. If a promise is breached, the law provides remedies to the harmed party, often in the form of monetary damages, or in limited circumstances, in the form of specific performance of the promise made. Elements -- Consideration and Mutual Assent Contracts arise when a duty comes into existence, because of a promise made by one of the parties. To be legally binding as a contract, a promise must be exchanged for adequate consideration. There are two different theories or definitions of consideration: Bargain Theory of Consideration and Benefit-Detriment theory of consideration. Benefit-Detriment Under the b enefit-detriment theory, an adequate consideration exists only when a promise made to the benefit of the promisor or to the detriment of the promisee, which reasonably and fairly induces the promisor to make a promise for something else for the promisee. For example, promises that are purely gifts are not considered enforceable because the personal satisfaction the grantor of the promise may receive from the act of generosity is normally not considered sufficient detriment to constitute adequate consideration. Bargain-for-Exchange Under Bargain-for-Exchange theory of consideration, adequate consideration exists when a promisor makes a promise in return for something else. Here, the essential condition is that the promisor was given something specifically to induce the promise being made. In other words, the bargain for exchange theory is different from the detriment-benefit theory in that the focus in bargain for exchange theory seems to be the parties’ motive for making the promises and the parties’ subjective mutual assent, while in detriment benefit theory, the focus seems to be an objective legal detriment or benefit to the parties. Governing Laws Contracts are mainly governed by state statutory and common (judge-made) law and private law (i.e. the private agreement). Private law principally includes the terms of the agreement between the parties who are exchanging promises. This private law may override many of the rules otherwise established by state law. Statutory law, such as the Statute of Fraud , may require some kinds of contracts be put in writing and executed with particular formalities, for the contract to be enforceable. Otherwise, the parties may enter into a binding agreement without signing a formal written document. For example, Virginia Supreme Court has held in Lucy v. Zehmer that even an agreement made on a piece of napkin can be considered a valid contract, if the parties were both sane, and showed mutual assent and consideration. Most of the principles of the common law of contracts are outlined in the Restatement of Law, Second Contracts published by the American Law Institute. The Uniform Commercial Code , whose original articles have been adopted in nearly every state, represents a body of statutory law that governs important categories of contracts. The main articles that deal with the law of contracts are Article 1 (General Provisions) and Article 2 (Sales) . Sections of Article 9 (
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes liability for direct damages, contradicting the basic principle of contract law. The provided snippet from LII/Legal Information Institute states that possible remedies for breach of contract include 'general damages, consequential damages, reliance damages, and specific performance'. By eliminating liability for direct damages, the changed text contradicts the provision of remedies to the harmed party, rendering the contract potentially unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: 13.3 This Agreement shall in all respects be governed by the laws of the Republic of South Africa .
perturbation.changed_text: 13.3 This Agreement shall in all respects be governed by the laws of Atlantis.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to that of a fictional nation renders the clause nonsensical and unenforceable, creating uncertainty about the applicable legal framework.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Principles of Private International Law and Choice of Law Rules.
perturbation.law_citation: Hague Principles on Choice of Law in International Commercial Contracts
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text specifies that the law of 'Atlantis,' a fictional location, governs the agreement. This is contrary to the established principles of private international law and choice-of-law rules, which require the selected jurisdiction to have a reasonable connection to the parties or the transaction. Choosing a nonexistent jurisdiction renders the governing law clause unenforceable, leading to uncertainty and potential legal challenges.

perturbation.justification: The modified text replaces a real jurisdiction (South Africa) with a fictional one (Atlantis). The Law Explanation states this is contrary to choice-of-law rules requiring a 'reasonable connection to the parties or the transaction.' The Perturbation Explanation further states that using a fictional nation renders the clause nonsensical and unenforceable. This aligns with the 'Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction' definition because the change deviates from legally recognized jurisdictions, making the contract unenforceable.
